card.intuition: Run DFS; when you finish exploring a node (all descendants done), prepend it to the result. The last node to finish has no outgoing edges, so it naturally ends up last — reading results forward gives a valid topological order.
card.analogy: Packing for a trip: you can only pack an item after packing everything it depends on. When you're done packing a bag (all sub-items inside), put the bag on the shelf. The order bags land on the shelf is the reverse finish order — a valid packing sequence.
when_to_use: Topological sort as a side-effect of DFS (e.g., during SCC computation)
When you already have a DFS framework and want to avoid a separate BFS pass
Generating all valid orderings via backtracking variants
gotchas: Use a 3-color state: WHITE (unvisited), GRAY (in-progress), BLACK (done) — a GRAY neighbour means a back-edge and a cycle
Append to result on node finish, then reverse — or prepend directly
This DFS-based sort does not detect cycles as cleanly as Kahn's — need explicit cycle tracking

exercise.prompt: Implement DFS topological sort using 3-colour cycle detection.
exercise.starter_code:
def topological_sort_dfs(n, adj):
    """
    n   : number of vertices (0..n-1)
    adj : adjacency list dict {u: [v, ...]}
    Returns topological order, or [] if cycle detected.
    """
    WHITE, GRAY, BLACK = 0, 1, 2
    color  = [WHITE] * n
    result = []
    cycle  = [False]

    def dfs(u):
        if cycle[0]: return
        color[u] = GRAY
        for v in adj.get(u, []):
            if color[v] == GRAY:      # back-edge -> cycle
                cycle[0] = True; return
            if color[v] == WHITE:
                dfs(v)
        color[u] = BLACK
        ___________                   # record finish

    for u in range(n):
        if color[u] == WHITE:
            dfs(u)

    return [] if cycle[0] else result[::-1]
hints: Append u to result when the node is fully finished (colored BLACK). Reversing result at the end gives the topological order.
